latest.title: Net Asset Value(s)
latest.content:
10 Jun 2026 11:53
RNS Number : 7871H
JPMorgan Emerging EMEA Securities
10 June 2026
JPMORGAN EMERGING EUROPE, MIDDLE EAST & AFRICA SECURITIES PLC (the 'Company')
Legal Entity Identifier: 549300II3MHI98ZLVH37
THE COMPANY ANNOUNCES THE UNAUDITED NET ASSET VALUE (NAV) As at: 09 June 2026
THE NET ASSET VALUE PER SHARE IN PENCE, INCLUDING INCOME WITH DEBT AT FAIR VALUE:
67.08
The Directors consider that in the absence of observable market data on its Russian investments resulting from the closure of the Moscow Exchange (MOEX) to overseas investors, there has been a material change to the market value of its Russian investments and therefore a fair value valuation methodology has been applied to those investments held. For its MOEX local stock, an alternative fair value adjustment has been applied to the last trade price on 25th February 2022. The price of these stocks has been determined by taking the live market price as at 25th February 2022 and applying a 99% provision for valuation. Similarly, for the American Depositary Receipts and Global Depositary Receipts an alternative fair value adjustment has been applied to the last trade price on 2nd March 2022 and a 99% provision for valuation applied.
Name of contact and telephone number for queries:
Paul Ainger 0044 207 742 6524
Name of authorised company official responsible for making this notification:
Paul Winship 0044 207 742 9815 - Company Secretary
Date: 10 June 2026

latest.title: Russian Court Cases - Update
latest.content:
8 Jun 2026 17:04
RNS Number : 4623H
JPMorgan Emerging EMEA Securities
08 June 2026
LONDON STOCK EXCHANGE ANNOUNCEMENT
JPMORGAN EMERGING EUROPE, MIDDLE EAST & AFRICA SECURITIES PLC
(the 'Company' or 'JEMA')
Legal Entity Identifier: 549300II3MHI98ZLVH37
Russian Court Cases - Update
Further to the Board's previous RNS announcements regarding the lawsuits filed by VTB Bank in Russia claiming $439.5m, $81.3 million, $74.5 million and €108 million and the lawsuit filed by Sberbank in Russia claiming $830,183 against eight J.P. Morgan legal entities and the Company, the Board announces the following updates from JPMorgan Funds Limited (the Company's Manager) (
in italics
):
VTB Bank claims ($81.3 million, $74.5 million and €108 million):
Claims withdrawn.
Further to the announcement on 16th February 2026, on 3rd June 2026 and 8th June 2026 respectively, the Russian Court accepted VTB Bank's motion to withdraw the claims in response to the Anti-Suit Injunction granted in the English Court and cancelled the associated freezing orders over JPM's assets.
VTB Bank claim ($439.5 million):
Appeal filed to a higher court, the Court of Cassation in Russia, is currently suspended.
The Court of Cassation has indicated that it does not intend to schedule a further hearing until the appeals filed by the relevant J.P. Morgan legal entities to the Supreme Court in a separate claim unrelated to the Company have been considered.
Sberbank claim ($830,183):
Hearing date now 13th July 2026.
The Board will provide further updates as the cases progress.
8th June 2026
For further information:
Joel Clopon, Neil Martin, JPMorgan Funds Limited 0800 20 40 20
Hugh Jonathan, Deutsche Numis 020 7260 1000
This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. company_30s.what_they_do: JEMA is a UK‑listed investment vehicle that manages a portfolio of assets, primarily in emerging markets. Its core activity is buying and holding securities, and it distributes dividends to shareholders.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The firm taps into a large pool of capital that seeks higher returns outside the UK market. By holding a mix of equities and bonds, it offers investors a way to diversify into regions that are often under‑represented in domestic portfolios. Its scale and focus on emerging markets give it a niche advantage.
company_30s.current_state: JEMA has been in a steep decline for several months, with its share price hovering near the bottom of its recent range. The company has released several updates on its net asset value and holdings, indicating that it is still operating and adjusting its portfolio. The latest news shows it is dealing with Russian court cases and sanctions, suggesting it is actively managing risk in its holdings.

rns_narrative: The company has been publishing a series of routine reports: a net asset value update, a list of its largest holdings, a closed‑period notice, and a status update on Russian court cases. Together, these show that JEMA is still functioning, refining its portfolio, and monitoring regulatory developments.

A second theme is the company’s exposure to Russian assets. The court case update and several investor posts point to ongoing legal scrutiny, while the NAV posts hint that the firm is trying to keep its book value transparent amid sanctions pressure.

The third theme is investor engagement: a handful of posts discuss potential deal news and the possibility of new funding or a relief from sanctions. This suggests that a catalyst could emerge from either a regulatory decision or a new investment opportunity.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet but hopeful – investors are waiting for a breakthrough in sanctions or a new investment that could lift the firm.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: Bullish chatter is centered on the idea that once sanctions ease, JEMA’s holdings could appreciate sharply. Some posts mention a potential deal that would boost the firm’s NAV and provide fresh capital.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Bears point to the unresolved Russian court cases and the risk that sanctions could worsen. They warn that the company’s portfolio may be heavily exposed to assets that could be frozen or devalued.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The community has moved from a neutral stance to a cautious optimism. While there is no active debate, the tone has shifted slightly toward expecting a positive catalyst.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends for JEMA is flat – no recent spikes in public interest.

near_term_catalysts: A fresh net asset value report that shows a jump in the book value could signal a hidden recovery.
Any announcement that sanctions on Russian assets are being lifted or that the court case is resolved would remove a key risk.
If the company announces a new investment or partnership that adds value to its portfolio, it could spark buying interest.
A sharp increase in trading volume, especially on a day when the price touches a key support level, would confirm that buyers are stepping in.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a sudden spike in volume, a price move above the 20‑day moving average, or a news release that updates the NAV or resolves a legal issue. These are the signals that would confirm the mean‑reversion thesis or, if absent, signal a stall.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock is still in the early stages of its crash cycle – the price is well below its historical high, and the market has largely ignored it. The APEX score indicates a tight compression, meaning a breakout could happen soon if a catalyst arrives.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: The share price is 86 % below its all‑time high, meaning it sits at roughly 14 % of that peak. If it recovers to half the peak (50 %), that is a 3.6‑fold jump, or a 260 % upside from today. The risk of a complete wipe‑out is limited to the current price itself, giving a risk/reward ratio of about 1 : 2.6. Historically, deep crashes of 70‑95 % have produced massive rebounds, so the asymmetry is highly attractive.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: If the Russian court cases are not resolved and sanctions tighten, the company could see its holdings frozen or devalued. The worst‑case scenario is a further decline to zero, wiping out the investment. The downside remains capped at the current price, but the probability of a full collapse is higher if legal risks materialise.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: You could lose 100 % of your investment if the price collapses to zero, but you stand to gain more than double that if the price rebounds to 50 % of its all‑time high. The potential reward far outweighs the risk in a deep‑crash environment.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter when the price closes above the 20‑day moving average after a period of consolidation. Use a position size that limits your exposure to 5 % of your portfolio. Place a stop 10 % below the entry to protect against a false breakout.
playbook.if_watching: Hold off until you see either a new NAV release that shows a significant jump, a court ruling that clears the company’s Russian assets, or a volume spike of at least 20 % above the 50‑day average. Those events would be the green light to buy.
playbook.if_passing: If the company announces a delisting, a bankruptcy filing, or a complete loss of its Russian holdings, exit immediately.

ai_insights.bottom_line: JEMA is a deeply crashed investment vehicle that could deliver more than 250 % upside if a sanctions‑related catalyst lifts its holdings, making it a high‑asymmetry contrarian play.
